title_en: Sample Entries and the Depth of Coverage
content_id:
Rentang entri dalam Al-Isabah menggambarkan baik kelengkapan karya ini maupun keragaman bukti yang Ibn Hajar evaluasi dalam menentukan status kesahabahan. Beberapa contoh representatif menunjukkan bagaimana karya ini berfungsi dalam praktik.

Untuk seorang Sahabat utama seperti Abu Hurairah, entrinya sangat ekstensif: Ibn Hajar menelusuri perdebatan tentang nama aslinya (beberapa nama dilaporkan dalam sumber-sumber), mendokumentasikan konversinya dan tahun-tahunnya bersama Nabi, mencatat jumlah hadis yang dikaitkan dengannya (salah satu yang terbesar dari Sahabat mana pun), menyebut murid-murid utamanya, dan mengevaluasi bukti untuk berbagai klaim tentang kehidupannya. Entri untuk Abu Hurairah menyarikan sejumlah besar literatur biografi dan kritik hadis menjadi sebuah catatan yang koheren.

Untuk Sahabat minor — tokoh yang hanya muncul dalam satu hadis atau yang namanya tersimpan hanya dalam satu sanad — entrinya mungkin singkat: beberapa baris mendokumentasikan bukti status kesahabahan dan riwayat-riwayat apa pun yang dikaitkan dengan tokoh tersebut. Entri-entri singkat untuk Sahabat yang tidak dikenal ini sering kali merupakan satu-satunya perlakuan sistematis terhadap tokoh tersebut di seluruh literatur klasik.

Untuk kasus-kasus yang diperdebatkan — individu yang diklaim sebagai Sahabat oleh beberapa sumber tetapi diperdebatkan oleh sumber lain — Ibn Hajar memeriksa bukti-bukti dengan cermat dan mencapai kesimpulan yang beralasan sambil mencatat ketidaksepakatan tersebut. Entri-entri ini sangat berharga karena menunjukkan proses analitik yang sedang bekerja: memeriksa klaim yang bersaing, mengevaluasi bukti untuk masing-masingnya, dan mencapai penilaian yang membedakan apa yang dapat ditetapkan dari apa yang masih belum pasti.

Entri-entri untuk Sahabat perempuan patut mendapat perhatian karena kehati-hatian dan kelengkapannya. Ibn Hajar mencakup biografi terperinci para Sahabat perempuan utama — Khadijah, Aisyah, Fatimah, Umm Salamah, dan banyak lainnya — serta entri-entri lebih pendek untuk ratusan perempuan yang kurang dikenal yang memiliki kontak apa pun yang tercatat dengan Nabi. Perhatian sistematis terhadap Sahabat perempuan ini mencerminkan pentingnya perempuan dalam transmisi pengetahuan kenabian dan status formal mereka sebagai anggota generasi Sahabat.
content_en:
The range of entries in Al-Isabah illustrates both the work's comprehensiveness and the variety of evidence that Ibn Hajar evaluated in determining companion status. A few representative examples show how the work functions in practice.

For a major companion like Abu Hurayrah, the entry is extensive: Ibn Hajar traces the debate about his real name (multiple names are reported in the sources), documents his conversion and his years with the Prophet, records the number of hadiths attributed to him (one of the largest of any companion), names his major students, and evaluates the evidence for various claims about his life. The entry for Abu Hurayrah distills a vast amount of biographical and hadith-critical literature into a coherent account.

For a minor companion — a figure who appears only in a single hadith or whose name is preserved only in a single chain of transmission — the entry may be brief: a few lines documenting the evidence for companion status and any reports attributed to the figure. These brief entries for obscure companions are often the only systematic treatment of that figure anywhere in classical literature.

For contested cases — individuals claimed as companions by some sources but disputed by others — Ibn Hajar examines the evidence carefully and reaches a reasoned conclusion while noting the disagreement. These entries are particularly valuable because they show the analytical process at work: examining competing claims, evaluating the evidence for each, and reaching a judgment that distinguishes what can be established from what remains uncertain.

The entries for women companions are notable for their care and completeness. Ibn Hajar includes detailed biographies of the major female companions — Khadijah, Aisha, Fatimah, Umm Salamah, and many others — and shorter entries for the hundreds of lesser-known women who had any recorded contact with the Prophet. This systematic attention to women companions reflects the importance of women in the transmission of prophetic knowledge and their formal status as members of the companion generation.
content_ar:
The range of entries in Al-Isabah illustrates both the work's comprehensiveness and the variety of evidence that Ibn Hajar evaluated in determining companion status. A few representative examples show how the work functions in practice.

For a major companion like Abu Hurayrah, the entry is extensive: Ibn Hajar traces the debate about his real name (multiple names are reported in the sources), documents his conversion and his years with النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم, records the number of hadiths attributed to him (one of the largest of any companion), names his major students, and evaluates the evidence for various claims about his life. The entry for Abu Hurayrah distills a vast amount of biographical and hadith-critical literature into a coherent account.

For a minor companion — a figure who appears only in a single hadith or whose name is preserved only in a single chain of transmission — the entry may be brief: a few lines documenting the evidence for companion status and any reports attributed to the figure. These brief entries for obscure companions are often the only systematic treatment of that figure anywhere in classical literature.

For contested cases — individuals claimed as companions by some sources but disputed by others — Ibn Hajar examines the evidence carefully and reaches a reasoned conclusion while noting the disagreement. These entries are particularly valuable because they show the analytical process at work: examining competing claims, evaluating the evidence for each, and reaching a judgment that distinguishes what can be established from what remains uncertain.

The entries for women companions are notable for their care and completeness. Ibn Hajar includes detailed biographies of the major female companions — Khadijah, Aisha, Fatimah, Umm Salamah, and many others — and shorter entries for the hundreds of lesser-known women who had any recorded contact with النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم. This systematic attention to women companions reflects the importance of women in the transmission of prophetic knowledge and their formal status as members of the companion generation.
